sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: A biochemist studies the citric acid (TCA/Krebs) cycle in isolated mitochondria. She traces the fate of acetyl-CoA as it enters the cycle by condensing with oxaloacetate (OAA) to form citrate. She monitors NADH and FADH₂ production, GTP generation by substrate-level phosphorylation, and CO₂ release. She also investigates key regulatory enzymes: citrate synthase, isocitrate dehydrogenase, and α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, and studies how the cycle integrates with gluconeogenesis, fatty acid synthesis, and amino acid metabolism. In a separate assay, she measures oxygen consumption in isolated mitochondria following sequential additions of substrates and inhibitors. The results are shown in Figure 1.
questions.stem: Per turn of the TCA cycle (one acetyl-CoA), the products are:

choices.label: A
choices.text: 3 NADH, 1 FADH₂, 1 GTP, and 2 CO₂ — CO₂ is released at the isocitrate dehydrogenase and α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase steps, though the acetyl-CoA carbons are not the ones lost as CO₂ in the first turn

choices.label: B
choices.text: 1 NADH, 1 FADH₂, 1 ATP, and 1 CO₂

choices.label: C
choices.text: 10 NADH, 2 FADH₂, and no CO₂

choices.label: D
choices.text: no reduced electron carriers and 6 CO₂

explanations.A: Correct. Per acetyl-CoA: 3 NADH (isocitrate dehydrogenase, α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, malate dehydrogenase), 1 FADH₂ (succinate dehydrogenase/Complex II), 1 GTP (succinyl-CoA synthetase — substrate-level phosphorylation, equivalent to 1 ATP via nucleoside diphosphate kinase), and 2 CO₂ (isocitrate → α-ketoglutarate, and α-ketoglutarate → succinyl-CoA). The two carbons lost as CO₂ are not the same carbons that entered as acetyl-CoA in that turn — isotope labeling studies show they are from OAA. Per glucose: 2 acetyl-CoA → 2 turns → 6 NADH, 2 FADH₂, 2 GTP, 4 CO₂.
explanations.B: This significantly underestimates the yield. The TCA cycle produces 3 NADH per turn (not 1) and 2 CO₂ (not 1). The cycle is the major source of reduced electron carriers that feed the ETC.
explanations.C: 10 NADH per turn is far too high. That is approximately the total NADH from all of glucose oxidation (glycolysis + pyruvate decarboxylation + 2 TCA turns). Each turn produces 3 NADH.
explanations.D: The TCA cycle is the primary producer of NADH and FADH₂ for oxidative phosphorylation. It generates 4 reduced carriers per turn. And 2 CO₂ are released, not 6.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Oxygen consumption rate of isolated mitochondria following substrate and inhibitor additions.

questions.stem: Oxaloacetate (OAA) is regenerated at the end of each TCA cycle turn. If OAA is diverted to gluconeogenesis, the cycle would:

choices.label: A
choices.text: speed up because less OAA means faster citrate synthesis

choices.label: B
choices.text: slow down — OAA is required to condense with acetyl-CoA (citrate synthase); its removal depletes the cycle intermediate pool, reducing flux through the cycle unless OAA is replenished by anaplerotic reactions

choices.label: C
choices.text: not be affected because OAA plays no catalytic role

choices.label: D
choices.text: switch to producing only FADH₂ without NADH

explanations.A: Less OAA would slow, not speed, citrate synthesis. Citrate synthase requires both acetyl-CoA and OAA as substrates — reducing either slows the reaction.
explanations.B: Correct. OAA is a catalytic intermediate — it is consumed and regenerated each turn but is not a net product. Draining OAA for gluconeogenesis (via PEPCK: OAA → PEP) depletes the cycle pool. Anaplerotic reactions replenish intermediates: pyruvate carboxylase (pyruvate + CO₂ → OAA, biotin-dependent, activated by acetyl-CoA) is the primary anaplerotic enzyme. Amino acid catabolism also feeds intermediates (glutamate → α-ketoglutarate, aspartate → OAA). Without anaplerosis, cycle flux drops and acetyl-CoA accumulates.
explanations.C: OAA is absolutely essential — it is the acceptor for acetyl-CoA in the first step (citrate synthase). It has a catalytic role: consumed at step 1, regenerated at step 8 (malate dehydrogenase). Removing it directly impairs cycle function.
explanations.D: The relative production of NADH and FADH₂ is determined by the specific dehydrogenase reactions, not by OAA availability. Depleting OAA would reduce overall flux, affecting all products proportionally.

questions.stem: The TCA cycle is regulated primarily at three irreversible steps. Isocitrate dehydrogenase, a key regulatory enzyme, is activated by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: high ATP and NADH levels

choices.label: B
choices.text: high citrate concentrations only

choices.label: C
choices.text: ADP and NAD⁺ — these signal low energy charge and oxidized carrier availability, stimulating the cycle to produce more NADH and GTP

choices.label: D
choices.text: oxygen deprivation (anaerobic conditions)

explanations.A: High ATP and NADH inhibit isocitrate dehydrogenase (and other TCA enzymes). These are signals of high energy charge — they indicate the cell has sufficient energy and does not need to run the cycle faster. This is product inhibition / energy-charge regulation.
explanations.B: Citrate is a product of the first step (citrate synthase) and can allosterically inhibit PFK-1 in glycolysis, but it is not the primary activator of isocitrate dehydrogenase.
explanations.C: Correct. TCA cycle regulation matches energy demand: the three regulated enzymes are citrate synthase, isocitrate dehydrogenase, and α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase. All three are inhibited by their products (NADH, succinyl-CoA, citrate, ATP) and activated by signals of low energy charge (ADP, NAD⁺, Ca²⁺). Isocitrate dehydrogenase: activated by ADP, NAD⁺, Ca²⁺; inhibited by ATP, NADH. When ATP is consumed (→ ADP rises) and NADH is oxidized by the ETC (→ NAD⁺ rises), the cycle accelerates to replenish energy carriers.
explanations.D: Anaerobic conditions would impair the TCA cycle because the ETC cannot oxidize NADH and FADH₂ without O₂. NADH accumulates, inhibiting the cycle's dehydrogenases. The cycle slows under anaerobic conditions, not accelerates.

questions.stem: Acetyl-CoA cannot be used for net glucose synthesis in animals because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: acetyl-CoA is too small to enter the TCA cycle

choices.label: B
choices.text: acetyl-CoA is directly converted to glucose by glucokinase

choices.label: C
choices.text: glucose is never needed by animal cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: the pyruvate dehydrogenase reaction is irreversible, and the two carbons entering the TCA cycle as acetyl-CoA are lost as CO₂ — there is no net pathway from acetyl-CoA to OAA or other gluconeogenic precursors in animals

explanations.A: Acetyl-CoA readily enters the TCA cycle by condensing with OAA (citrate synthase). Size is not the issue — the issue is the irreversibility of the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex and the stoichiometry of the TCA cycle.
explanations.B: Glucokinase phosphorylates glucose (glucose → G6P), the first step of glycolysis. It does not produce glucose from acetyl-CoA. Gluconeogenesis uses PEP carboxykinase, fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase, and glucose-6-phosphatase.
explanations.C: Glucose is essential for animal cells — the brain alone consumes ~120 g/day. The issue is not whether glucose is needed but whether it can be synthesized from acetyl-CoA (it cannot in animals).
explanations.D: Correct. Two barriers prevent net gluconeogenesis from acetyl-CoA: (1) Pyruvate dehydrogenase (pyruvate → acetyl-CoA + CO₂) is physiologically irreversible — there is no animal enzyme that converts acetyl-CoA back to pyruvate. (2) In the TCA cycle, the two carbons entering as acetyl-CoA are eventually lost as CO₂ (at isocitrate dehydrogenase and α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase). OAA is regenerated but not in excess — no net conversion of acetyl-CoA carbons to OAA for gluconeogenesis. This is why fatty acids (which yield only acetyl-CoA via β-oxidation) cannot be converted to glucose in animals. Plants and bacteria can do this via the glyoxylate cycle.

questions.stem: α-Ketoglutarate dehydrogenase is mechanistically similar to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: DNA polymerase

choices.label: B
choices.text: hexokinase

choices.label: C
choices.text: the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex — both are multi-enzyme complexes that catalyze oxidative decarboxylation using the same five coenzymes: TPP, lipoamide, CoA, FAD, and NAD⁺

choices.label: D
choices.text: catalase

explanations.A: DNA polymerase synthesizes DNA by adding nucleotides. It has no mechanistic similarity to a dehydrogenase complex.
explanations.B: Hexokinase phosphorylates glucose (glucose + ATP → G6P + ADP). It is a transferase, not a multi-enzyme dehydrogenase complex.
explanations.C: Correct. Both pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDC) and α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase complex (α-KGDC) catalyze oxidative decarboxylation of an α-keto acid: PDC: pyruvate → acetyl-CoA + CO₂ + NADH; α-KGDC: α-ketoglutarate → succinyl-CoA + CO₂ + NADH. Both use the same five coenzymes: TPP (thiamine pyrophosphate, vitamin B₁), lipoic acid (lipoamide), CoA (pantothenic acid, B₅), FAD (riboflavin, B₂), and NAD⁺ (niacin, B₃). This is a favorite MCAT connection — deficiency of any of these vitamins impairs both reactions.
explanations.D: Catalase decomposes H₂O₂ → H₂O + O₂. It is a heme-containing oxidoreductase with no structural or mechanistic similarity to a multi-enzyme dehydrogenase complex.

questions.passage: The biochemist studies metabolic integration by examining how the TCA cycle connects to fatty acid synthesis, amino acid catabolism, and the urea cycle. She notes that citrate exported from mitochondria provides cytoplasmic acetyl-CoA for fatty acid synthesis (via ATP-citrate lyase), that transamination reactions interconvert amino acids and TCA intermediates, and that the malate-aspartate shuttle transfers reducing equivalents between compartments.
questions.stem: Citrate exported to the cytoplasm is cleaved by ATP-citrate lyase to provide:

choices.label: A
choices.text: acetyl-CoA and oxaloacetate — the acetyl-CoA serves as the building block for fatty acid synthesis, and the OAA can be recycled back to malate for mitochondrial re-entry

choices.label: B
choices.text: two molecules of pyruvate

choices.label: C
choices.text: glucose-6-phosphate directly

choices.label: D
choices.text: succinyl-CoA for heme synthesis

explanations.A: Correct. The citrate shuttle: when the TCA cycle has excess citrate (high energy charge → isocitrate dehydrogenase inhibited → citrate accumulates), citrate is exported to the cytoplasm via the tricarboxylate transporter. ATP-citrate lyase cleaves it: citrate + CoA + ATP → acetyl-CoA + OAA + ADP + Pi. Cytoplasmic acetyl-CoA is then carboxylated to malonyl-CoA (by ACC, acetyl-CoA carboxylase, the committed step) and used for fatty acid synthesis. The OAA is reduced to malate (cytoplasmic malate dehydrogenase) and either re-enters the mitochondria or is oxidized to pyruvate (malic enzyme, generating NADPH for fatty acid synthesis).
explanations.B: ATP-citrate lyase does not produce pyruvate. It specifically cleaves citrate into acetyl-CoA and OAA. Pyruvate can be generated from OAA indirectly (OAA → malate → pyruvate via malic enzyme).
explanations.C: Glucose-6-phosphate is produced by hexokinase (from glucose) or glucose-6-phosphatase (from G6P in gluconeogenesis). It is not a product of citrate cleavage.
explanations.D: Succinyl-CoA for heme synthesis is produced in the mitochondria (from the TCA cycle or from α-ketoglutarate). ATP-citrate lyase produces acetyl-CoA, not succinyl-CoA.

questions.topic: Citrate Shuttle / Fatty Acid Synthesis Link

questions.stem: Transamination reactions connect amino acid metabolism to the TCA cycle by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: irreversibly destroying all amino acid carbon skeletons

choices.label: B
choices.text: transferring the amino group from an amino acid to an α-keto acid, producing a new amino acid and a new α-keto acid — this links amino acid catabolism to TCA intermediates like α-ketoglutarate and oxaloacetate

choices.label: C
choices.text: converting all amino acids directly into acetyl-CoA without any intermediate steps

choices.label: D
choices.text: producing urea without involving any TCA cycle intermediate

explanations.A: Transamination is reversible and does not destroy carbon skeletons. It redistributes amino groups between molecules. The carbon skeletons can enter the TCA cycle (glucogenic amino acids → OAA, α-KG, succinyl-CoA, fumarate; ketogenic amino acids → acetyl-CoA, acetoacetyl-CoA).
explanations.B: Correct. Transamination: amino acid + α-keto acid ⇌ new α-keto acid + new amino acid. Catalyzed by aminotransferases (transaminases) using pyridoxal phosphate (PLP, vitamin B₆) as coenzyme. Key reactions: alanine + α-ketoglutarate ⇌ pyruvate + glutamate (ALT/GPT); aspartate + α-ketoglutarate ⇌ oxaloacetate + glutamate (AST/GOT). These reactions funnel amino groups into glutamate, which can then undergo oxidative deamination (glutamate dehydrogenase: glutamate → α-ketoglutarate + NH₄⁺) to release free ammonia for the urea cycle. The carbon skeletons enter the TCA cycle or gluconeogenesis.
explanations.C: Not all amino acids produce acetyl-CoA. Glucogenic amino acids (most amino acids) produce TCA intermediates or pyruvate. Only leucine and lysine are purely ketogenic (acetyl-CoA only). Several amino acids are both glucogenic and ketogenic.
explanations.D: The urea cycle does involve TCA intermediates: fumarate (produced by argininosuccinate lyase in the urea cycle) enters the TCA cycle, and aspartate (from OAA transamination) is a urea cycle substrate. The two cycles are linked at these points (the 'Krebs bicycle').

questions.stem: The malate-aspartate shuttle transfers NADH reducing equivalents from the cytoplasm to the mitochondrial matrix because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: NADH freely crosses the inner mitochondrial membrane

choices.label: B
choices.text: the shuttle converts NADH to ATP without involving the ETC

choices.label: C
choices.text: FADH₂ is the only reduced carrier produced in the cytoplasm

choices.label: D
choices.text: the inner mitochondrial membrane is impermeable to NADH — the shuttle uses carrier molecules (malate and aspartate) to transfer electrons across the membrane, regenerating cytoplasmic NAD⁺ for continued glycolysis while delivering electrons to mitochondrial NAD⁺ for the ETC

explanations.A: NADH cannot cross the inner mitochondrial membrane — it is too large and charged. This is why shuttle systems exist. The membrane's impermeability to NADH (and NAD⁺) is essential for maintaining separate cytoplasmic and mitochondrial NAD⁺/NADH pools.
explanations.B: The shuttle transfers electrons, not ATP. The electrons ultimately reach the ETC (via mitochondrial NADH), which produces ATP through oxidative phosphorylation. The shuttle itself does not make ATP.
explanations.C: Glycolysis produces NADH (glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase), not FADH₂, in the cytoplasm. FADH₂ is produced in the mitochondria (succinate dehydrogenase in the TCA cycle, and also by the glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle's mitochondrial component).
explanations.D: Correct. Malate-aspartate shuttle: (1) Cytoplasmic OAA is reduced to malate by cytoplasmic malate dehydrogenase (using cytoplasmic NADH → NAD⁺). (2) Malate enters the mitochondria via the malate-α-ketoglutarate antiporter. (3) Mitochondrial malate is oxidized back to OAA by mitochondrial malate dehydrogenase (reducing mitochondrial NAD⁺ → NADH). (4) OAA is transaminated to aspartate, which exits the mitochondria. Net result: cytoplasmic NADH electrons are transferred to mitochondrial NADH, yielding 2.5 ATP per NADH via the ETC. This shuttle operates in liver, heart, and kidney. The alternative glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle (muscle, brain) transfers electrons to mitochondrial FADH₂, yielding only 1.5 ATP.

questions.stem: Fluoroacetate is a toxin that is converted to fluorocitrate, which inhibits aconitase (citrate → isocitrate). Inhibiting aconitase would cause:

choices.label: A
choices.text: accumulation of citrate and depletion of downstream TCA intermediates — the cycle stalls because citrate cannot be converted to isocitrate, blocking all subsequent reactions

choices.label: B
choices.text: increased NADH production from the cycle

choices.label: C
choices.text: accelerated flux through the entire cycle

choices.label: D
choices.text: no effect because aconitase is not part of the TCA cycle

explanations.A: Correct. Aconitase catalyzes citrate → isocitrate (via cis-aconitate). Blocking this step causes citrate to accumulate (no conversion to isocitrate) and all downstream intermediates to be depleted (α-ketoglutarate, succinyl-CoA, succinate, fumarate, malate, OAA are not regenerated). Without OAA regeneration, citrate synthase cannot run even if acetyl-CoA is available. The cycle effectively stops. Fluoroacetate poisoning (found in some toxic plants) is lethal because it halts aerobic metabolism — a classic example of 'lethal synthesis' (the toxin itself is harmless but is metabolically converted to the inhibitory species).
explanations.B: NADH production would decrease dramatically because three of the four NADH-producing steps are downstream of aconitase (isocitrate dehydrogenase, α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, malate dehydrogenase). With the cycle blocked, these steps cannot operate.
explanations.C: The cycle would decelerate and eventually stop, not accelerate. Blocking any step in a cyclic pathway prevents regeneration of intermediates needed for the cycle to continue.
explanations.D: Aconitase catalyzes step 2 of the TCA cycle (citrate → isocitrate). It is absolutely part of the cycle.

questions.stem: Succinate dehydrogenase is unique among TCA cycle enzymes because it:

choices.label: A
choices.text: is the only enzyme located in the cytoplasm

choices.label: B
choices.text: produces NADH instead of FADH₂

choices.label: C
choices.text: is embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane and also functions as Complex II of the ETC — it directly transfers electrons from succinate to ubiquinone via FAD, linking the TCA cycle and the ETC in a single enzyme complex

choices.label: D
choices.text: requires no coenzyme or prosthetic group

explanations.A: All TCA cycle enzymes except succinate dehydrogenase are soluble matrix enzymes. Succinate dehydrogenase is the exception — it is embedded in the inner membrane, where it serves as both a TCA enzyme and ETC Complex II.
explanations.B: Succinate dehydrogenase produces FADH₂, not NADH. It is the only TCA dehydrogenase that uses FAD (covalently bound) rather than NAD⁺. This is why FADH₂ yields fewer ATP (~1.5 vs ~2.5 for NADH) — electrons enter the ETC at Complex II (ubiquinone level) rather than Complex I.
explanations.C: Correct. Succinate dehydrogenase (SDH) = Complex II of the ETC. It catalyzes succinate → fumarate (TCA cycle step) while transferring electrons through FAD → iron-sulfur clusters → ubiquinone (ETC function). It is the only TCA enzyme that is an integral membrane protein and the only ETC complex that does not pump protons. Its dual role directly couples substrate oxidation in the TCA cycle to electron flow in the ETC without a soluble carrier intermediate.
explanations.D: SDH contains multiple prosthetic groups: covalently bound FAD, iron-sulfur clusters ([2Fe-2S], [4Fe-4S], [3Fe-4S]), and a heme b. It has one of the most complex cofactor arrays of any single enzyme.

questions.topic: Succinate Dehydrogenase / Complex II / Dual Function
